planting.note: Direct sow in fall in well-drained, lean, sandy or loamy soil in full sun for a late winter to spring bloom; self-sows prolifically where happy.

directions: Bird's-eye Gilia is a charming California native annual wildflower bearing masses of small, tubular, violet-purple flowers marked with a ring of deep purple and a yellow-orange throat — the three-toned pattern gives the species both its common name and its epithet tricolor. It is native to the dry, open grasslands and oak woodland clearings of California's Central Valley and Coast Ranges, where it blooms from February through May. Direct sow in fall in lean, well-drained, sandy or loamy soil in full sun; seeds require cool, moist conditions to germinate and will sprout through winter rains to bloom in late winter and spring. Bird's-eye Gilia self-sows prolifically in open, sunny, California-native garden beds and makes an excellent companion for California poppies and native clarkia.
sources: Las Pilitas Nursery — California Native Plant Guide

planting.note: Direct sow at or just after last frost in dry, lean, well-drained soil in full sun; self-sows moderately in open disturbed ground.

directions: Rothrock's Knapweed is a native annual or biennial of mountain meadows, roadsides, and open disturbed ground in Arizona, New Mexico, and adjacent Mexico — one of the few Centaurea species native to the southwestern United States. It produces showy, rose-purple, thistle-like composite flower heads on branching stems from summer through fall, attracting native bees, butterflies, and hummingbirds. Unlike many knapweeds in the genus, this native species is non-invasive and ecologically appropriate for southwestern US gardens. Direct sow in spring after last frost in dry, well-drained, lean soil in full sun; plants self-sow moderately and establish naturally in open, rocky, or disturbed ground.
sources: Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center — Native Plant Database

planting.note: Transplant in spring after danger of frost; scarify or soak seeds in hot water before sowing indoors to improve germination rates.

directions: Desert Globemallow is one of the showiest and most familiar wildflowers of the Sonoran and Mojave Deserts, forming a woody-based perennial mound of gray-green, densely hairy, lobed mallow leaves. It produces generous clusters of brilliant apricot-orange — occasionally rose-pink or red — bowl-shaped flowers 1 to 1.5 inches across from late winter through spring, and blooms again after summer monsoon rains in the Sonoran Desert. Exceptionally drought- and heat-tolerant, it thrives in rocky, gravelly desert soils with sharp drainage and minimal supplemental water once established. Scarify or soak seeds in hot water before starting indoors, then transplant into dry, lean, gravelly soil in full sun for best results in xeriscape and desert gardens across zones 7-11.
sources: Arizona-Sonora Desert Museum — Plant Care Guide

planting.note: Direct sow in fall after lightly scarifying seeds; cold winter conditions improve germination and establishment the following spring.

directions: Scarlet Globemallow is a low, spreading native perennial groundcover of dry, open grasslands, roadsides, and disturbed ground throughout the Great Plains and western United States, ranging from Canada south to Mexico. From May through August its stems are covered in bright scarlet-orange, 5-petaled mallow flowers 0.75 to 1 inch across, held on upright stems clothed in deeply lobed, gray-green, hairy leaves. Among the toughest of native perennials, it endures extreme drought, heat, and cold across a wide range from zones 3 to 9 and spreads gradually by rhizomes and root sprouts to form open colonies. Lightly scarify seeds before direct sowing in fall in lean, very well-drained, sandy or gravelly soil in full sun; established plants require virtually no supplemental water or care.
sources: Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center — Native Plant Database

planting.note: Scarify or hot-soak seeds before starting indoors; transplant in spring into dry, calcareous or sandy, lean, well-drained soil in full sun.

directions: Gray Globemallow is a robust, erect native perennial of dry, calcareous grasslands, rocky slopes, and roadsides in Texas and the southwestern United States, distinctive for its densely silver-gray woolly stems and broad, deeply lobed, gray-green leaves that remain ornamental even when the plant is not in flower. From spring through summer it produces upright spikes of salmon-orange to brick-red mallow flowers 1 inch across that are highly attractive to native bees and hummingbirds. Grow in dry, calcareous or well-drained sandy, lean soil in full sun; plants rot quickly in heavy, wet, or organically rich soils. Scarify or soak seeds in hot water before starting indoors, then transplant into the garden in spring — an outstanding xeriscape perennial for Texas Hill Country and southwestern gardens.
sources: Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center — Native Plant Database
